chapters.title: Today, technology runs our lives and pushes us toward constant surveillance
chapters.text:
In a world where technology touches nearly every part of daily life, the idea of privacy as a basic right can feel unrealistic. The thought of having your whole life watched, recorded, and used against you would bother almost anyone, and it bothered Edward Snowden.

New technology has brought more data collection under the cover of making life easier.

Most people do not worry about how invasive their devices really are. We use them and they use us back. Plenty of users do not know how their devices track them, how a phone knows the time, or how search results seem to reflect a conversation they had just minutes ago.

That kind of ignorance feeds a quiet form of technological control. The devices end up running the user, and the user's personal information along with them. Whenever a software update pops up, most of us tap through it and hand over whatever data is asked for, even when that information could be used against us later. The control builds slowly, and before long the user becomes a possession of the device they thought they owned.

To take back some sense of self and ownership, Snowden turned to hacking. Hacking means gaining unauthorized access to a computer, site, or network by working with code. Snowden believes hacking is one of the sanest, healthiest, and most educational ways for kids to claim independence and meet adults as equals.

To hack well, you need a real understanding of the rules that govern the system, site, or network, and you should not worry too much about the people who built it. Once you know the rules, you can explore what the system is meant to do and then push one or two steps beyond, tweaking it so it works differently than its creators intended.

That skill can be useful for repurposing a system, network, or site so it does more than it was built for. A cracker, by contrast, hacks a system with bad intent, either to steal data or to break the system.

chapters.title: Snowden's early years shaped him for the task ahead
chapters.text:
Growing up in the United States, one of the most common sources of childhood trauma was divorce. Snowden has talked about how his parents' divorce scarred him, and how that experience shaped the rest of his life.

Snowden argues that when people underestimate you, the right move is to ignore them. People who write you off are projecting their own limits onto you. Your strengths and weaknesses are yours to decide, and it is unwise to let someone else define them.

Letting someone else define you, especially when they are projecting their shortcomings, is a trap.

This kind of self-knowledge is a mark of maturity, even at a young age. It can come from years of living or from a single hard experience. Snowden recalls a line he heard once, that you are not really an adult until you marry a parent or become one. For a kid living through a divorce, both of those things happen at once. The parents who once felt like a solid defense are falling apart and scrambling for footing. The child has to toughen up and become a parent to themselves, and sometimes a parent to their parent.

When trauma pushes you inward, look for comfort in new relationships with people you respect and who respect you back.

The trauma will leave a mark, and it will shape your adult life. Just as you may gravitate toward people who share your oddities, a child with a hard past tends to find friends with similar pasts.

That kind of kinship is often found in places like the military, which is where Snowden found his crowd. Joining the army, much like joining a tribe, can lift your confidence and give you a sense of purpose, the way it did for Snowden, who found a duty to use his skills in service of his country.

A larger sense of patriotism, fed by the structure of institutions like the military, encourages honesty and removes the fear of being judged for old mistakes.

chapters.title: Technology has made the world more vulnerable, and that poses a real challenge for privacy
chapters.text:
As technology has advanced, the world's vulnerabilities have become harder to ignore. Some countries have tried to take advantage of those weak spots. Others have worked to defend themselves from attackers. Snowden saw this clearly during his time as a system administrator and system engineer, where he could see the full picture from the inside.

The networks and platforms built for gathering data have dovetailed neatly with the urge to spy on people.

While working in the intelligence community, Snowden ran into a string of practices that amounted to deliberate corruption.

The habit of handing work that government employees could do themselves to private contractors stood out the most. These contracts were a way to siphon public money into private hands with little oversight. Private contractors were not patriots. The well-being of the country was not their first concern. Whoever paid more could buy their loyalty. There was no real sense of duty or honor in the work.

Patriotism alone does not win modern wars, and the government understood that.

Snowden was discredited once it became clear he had been asking questions. The government had something to hide. Officials defended their actions, legal or not, as necessary for national security. They operated with an arrogance backed by powers that demanded secrecy from top to bottom. With intelligence agencies, the government tends to ship fixes for problems that may or may not exist, and the fixes go in without any pushback.

That kind of behavior tends to backfire. When you build units that answer to no one, they can turn on the people who created them. Some call this the Frankenstein effect. Another problem that bothered Snowden was the active spying the United States ran on other countries through their embassies. The data collected from other nations was often being used to mimic the very behavior those nations were accused of.

chapters.title: Privacy is a right that should never be up for debate, yet it keeps being taken for granted
chapters.text:
The privacy of the public should go untouched and should not even be a topic for debate. Our data privacy is our own responsibility to protect, and Snowden agrees. He urges anyone who does not yet see the value of privacy to wake up and reclaim their freedom.

It is poor logic to say that because you cannot speak, no one else should bother speaking. Speak up against the invasion of privacy.

It is worth noting that the US government, through the NSA, ran mass surveillance on a global scale. The operation used NASA satellites, was sanctioned, and was justified. It also involved reframing ordinary citizens' private internet communications as potential signals intelligence.

To be fair, technology itself does not carry a moral code. Technology tends to grow past moral, legal, and ethical lines because those limits only slow it down. That careless attitude is part of the problem.

Technology runs without a moral code, which leaves plenty of room for abuse.

Some argue that you cannot criticize technology for moving in a direction, whether right or wrong. The thinking goes that if something can be done, it has either been done or will be done. That kind of logic helped the United States justify reverse-engineering the tools of the countries it called enemies. It was also used to justify the harshest actions taken by the US government.

chapters.title: There must be a limit to what can be monitored or pulled from a user's online profile
chapters.text:
Where is the limit? How much of our information can we keep to ourselves? These are the questions anyone who cares about privacy eventually asks. Most people never get there. They scroll through their devices and hand over any detail that is asked for.

If you care about privacy, you will be angry to learn that there is no real cap on what government agencies can collect from your daily life.

That passivity makes it hard to keep intelligence gathering in check. Snowden, however, found a way to give something back. He pulled together information from public reader boards online and showed how those boards also tracked information users posted and filtered it for keywords.

He understood what the networks were built for and how they could be abused. The government was invading the privacy not just of foreign adversaries but of its own citizens.

He decided to become a whistleblower, but he had to do it the right way. He had to figure out what to disclose, who to disclose it to, and how to share it with them.

Finding the right balance between what you want to share and what needs to stay private is essential.

His years inside the NSA gave him everything he needed to pull it off. Snowden quietly copied and compressed files he had access to at the facility. He made multiple copies.

He took every precaution he could, knowing it would all be worth it if he could expose the secrets the government was trying to keep. He encrypted the files with strong codes, kept the key to himself, and covered his tracks in case he was caught.

chapters.title: Uncovering the truth about the government filled him with guilt, but Snowden knew it would matter
chapters.text:
By the end, Edward Snowden was carrying a growing weight of fear and guilt. What he had done looked like treason. He had already started putting his affairs in order in case he did not survive the process. Still, he felt driven to speak out, because he had proof that the government was the bad actor all along. Officials had spent years telling the public that the real enemy was across the border, but the real enemy was closer to home.

The US government poured money into staying ahead of the competition, and in the process made its own citizens less safe.

He knew that what he was doing could end his life, no matter how it played out. After the leaks became public, he sought asylum in Russia. He spent 40 days and nights unable to find a country willing to take him in. No one wanted to shelter a volcano.

Because of the heat around him, Snowden had a hard time finding any country that would grant him asylum.

The thing about volcanoes, though, is that they can either destroy or renew. Once the lava cools, it can enrich the soil, cool the planet, and build new islands. Snowden hoped that whatever unrest his leaks caused would eventually settle and turn into something the public did not know they needed.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Privacy is a right we should hold without interference, but modern technology has made it harder to keep information private. That was the problem that pushed Edward Snowden to act. He decided the world needed to know it was being watched and monitored through embassies and everyday gadgets. He wanted to remind people that they can choose how much information they share with the public.

Snowden not only shared valuable secrets with the public, he opened their eyes to rights they did not know they had. The government had spent years and billions of dollars on secret projects that seriously invaded the privacy of its own citizens. The forms people filled out were quietly being routed to the government in the name of public safety, but the public was not safer. The data was simply being watched, and the intelligence was used to fight wars outside the country's borders.

Snowden saw this as a serious overreach. He believed it was wrong for the government to make those calls without asking the people being watched. He set out to break the fourth wall and return to the people their right to a safer, more private life.

A practical step is to pay close attention to the amount and content of the information you share on social media. Many people give up sensitive details about themselves online without realizing it. You could become a target of unauthorized surveillance or phishing, which is the illegal harvesting of sensitive data from the internet.
